mnemonics.title: Store Operation Sequence
mnemonics.content: M-A-D-W: Memory address first, then Data, then Write signal. Think: 'Mothers Always Demand Warm food' - Remember: Address (MAR) → Data (MDR) → Write + Wait for MFC

mnemonics.title: Endianness Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: BIG-endian stores the BIG end first (most significant byte at lowest address). LITTLE-endian stores the LITTLE end first (least significant byte at lowest address). Visualize: Big End = Big Toe (first in memory) = Big end of word.

acronyms.usage: Holds the address of memory location to read from or write to
Holds data to be written to memory or data read from memory
Signal from memory indicating read/write operation has finished
Enables the memory write operation when asserted
Enables the memory read operation when asserted
Assembly instruction to store a word from register to memory

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with CPU in center. Fetch: Memory → Bus → MDR → Register. Store: Register → MDR → Bus → Memory. Arrow directions show data flow - FETCH goes INTO CPU, STORE goes OUT of CPU.

Draw horizontal timeline with clock cycles. Cycle 1: MAR←Addr (address on bus). Cycle 2: MDR←Data (data placed). Cycle 3: WR=1, wait for MFC. Show WR and MFC signals with proper timing - WR must be stable before MFC sampled.

For 0x12345678 at address 100:
Big: [100]=12 [101]=34 [102]=56 [103]=78 (like reading book left-to-right)
Little: [100]=78 [101]=56 [102]=34 [103]=12 (number reversed in memory)

analogies.analogy: Think of store like photocopying a document (register) and placing the copy in a file cabinet (memory). The original stays in the register - you're making a copy, not moving it.
Like a student taking notes in a notebook (cache) and only copying to the master file (memory) when the notebook is full and needs to be cleared for new notes.
Like a clerk who accepts completed forms (stores) quickly and files them later, rather than making customers wait while each form is physically filed.
